Go

Golang

Programming

Software Development

Backend Development

2 min read
Just now

--

My journey from C# and ASP.NET to exploring one of the fastest-growing programming languages
When I first heard about Go (also known as Golang), I didn’t pay much attention to it. As a Full Stack Developer, I had been working primarily with C#, ASP.NET, SQL Server, and JavaScript. Those technologies had helped me build web applications and solve real-world problems for years.
So I asked myself a simple question:
“Why should I learn another programming language?”
The answer became clearer the more I explored modern software development.
The Growing Demand for Go
Over the past few years, I noticed that many popular companies were using Go to build scalable systems and cloud-native applications. It kept appearing in job descriptions, open-source projects, and discussions about backend development.
That caught my attention.
Instead of learning Go because it was “trendy,” I wanted to understand what made developers choose it over other languages.
My First Impression
The first thing I noticed was how simple Go feels.
There aren’t dozens of ways to solve the same problem. The language encourages writing clean, readable code without unnecessary complexity.
Coming from C#, this simplicity was refreshing.
Instead of spending time understanding complicated syntax, I could focus on solving the actual problem.
What I Like About Go
Here are a few reasons that convinced me to continue learning it:

• Simple and easy-to-read syntax

• Excellent performance because it’s compiled

• Fast compilation times

• Built-in support for concurrency using goroutines

• Strong community and growing ecosystem

• Great choice for APIs, cloud applications, and microservices
My Learning Plan
Rather than rushing through tutorials, I decided to build a strong foundation.
My plan looks like this:

• Learn Go syntax and language fundamentals.

• Practice data structures and algorithms in Go.

• Build small console applications.

• Create REST APIs.

• Learn concurrency with goroutines and channels.

• Build real-world backend projects.

• Explore Docker and Kubernetes with Go.
I believe building projects is the fastest way to learn any programming language.
My Goal
Learning Go isn’t about replacing C#.
Instead, I want another powerful tool in my developer toolkit.
Every language has its strengths, and I believe Go is an excellent choice for building high-performance backend services and modern cloud applications.
As I continue learning, I’ll share my experiences, mistakes, and projects here on Medium.
Hopefully, someone else starting their Go journey will find these articles helpful.
Final Thoughts
Learning a new technology can feel overwhelming at first.
But every expert started as a beginner.
I’m excited to see where this journey takes me, and I’m looking forward to building my first real project in Go.
If you’re also learning Go, I’d love to hear what motivated you to start.
Happy coding!
